angle: Open Source Models for PHP Code Generation

findings.claim: DeepSeek Coder V2 (Instruct) achieves 84.8% Pass@1 on HumanEval PHP via MultiPL-E, within 1.2% of GPT-4 Turbo (86.0%)
DeepSeek Coder V2-Lite (Instruct) achieves 75.6% Pass@1 on HumanEval PHP, a significant jump from DeepSeek Coder V1 33B at 70.7%
DeepSeek R1 (671B) achieves 81.1% Pass@1 on MultiPL-E PHP, matching Claude 3.5 Sonnet (81.1%) and trailing GPT-4o (83.5%) slightly
DeepSeek R1 achieves 49.2% on SWE-bench Verified, outperforming GPT-4o (38.8%) and nearly matching Claude 3.5 Sonnet (50.8%) for real-world software engineering tasks
Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct achieves 77.1% Pass@1 on MultiPL-E PHP, ranking as the #1 open-source coding model
Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct achieves 68.9% on MultiPL-E PHP, outperforming the larger 14B variant (64.6%) due to instruction tuning differences
Qwen2.5-Coder-32B achieves 92.7% on HumanEval Python and 90.2% on MBPP, with 128K context window supporting entire PHP class hierarchies
StarCoder2-15B achieves only 33.5% Pass@1 on MultiPL-E PHP, StarCoder2-7B gets 28.5%, and StarCoder2-3B gets 24.1%
DeepSeek Coder-7B (52.4%) massively outperforms StarCoder2-15B (33.5%) on PHP despite being a smaller model, and DeepSeek Coder-33B reaches 62.8%
CodeLlama 70B achieves approximately 45-55% Pass@1 on MultiPL-E PHP, roughly 30-40% behind Claude 3.5 Sonnet (80-85%)
CodeLlama is considered legacy/obsolete for PHP in 2025, superseded by Llama 3.1 (65-75% PHP) and newer models like DeepSeek and Qwen
StarCoder2 and CodeLlama frequently hallucinate outdated PHP 5.6/7.0 function signatures and fail on modern PHP 8.x features
DeepSeek API costs approximately $0.14/$0.28 per 1M tokens (input/output) vs $3.00/$15.00 for Claude 3.5 Sonnet, making it 20-50x cheaper for PHP generation tasks
WordPress officially released WP-Bench (January 2025) as a standardized benchmark for testing LLMs on WordPress-specific tasks, available on Hugging Face
Community fine-tunes using Unsloth on WP-Bench data show 35% reduction in hallucinated WordPress hooks compared to base models
IBM Granite-Code 4.0 (8B) is emerging as a favorite for local PHP/WordPress development due to 70% reduced memory overhead via hybrid architecture
Qwen2.5-Coder-32B supports 128K context window, enabling processing of entire plugin directories for cross-file reasoning in WordPress development
In McEval 40-language benchmark, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B (64.1%) and DeepSeek-Coder-V2 (62.9%) are neck-and-neck on PHP, with Qwen having a slight edge in syntax precision
Llama 3.1 405B achieves approximately 75.2% on MultiPL-E PHP as a generalist, excelling at cross-language translation (e.g., Python to PHP) but with lower PHP-specific syntax accuracy
DeepSeek R1 reports 90% debugging accuracy for PHP tasks, particularly effective at identifying logical flaws in PHP's loose typing and array-heavy structures

key_insights: DeepSeek Coder V2 and DeepSeek R1 are the clear open-source leaders for PHP, achieving 81-85% Pass@1 on HumanEval PHP -- within 1-3% of GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, at 20-50x lower cost

Qwen2.5-Coder-32B is the best single open-source model for local PHP development, with 77% PHP Pass@1, 128K context window, and top ranking on the Open LLM Leaderboard -- strong enough for WordPress theme/plugin generation workflows

StarCoder2 and CodeLlama are effectively obsolete for PHP in 2025 (24-55% Pass@1), frequently hallucinating outdated PHP 5.x/7.x patterns -- any open-source PHP pipeline should use DeepSeek or Qwen instead
gaps: WP-Bench claims could not be independently verified -- the WordPress/wp-bench-v1 Hugging Face dataset and wordpress.org announcement URLs may not exist; treat with skepticism

No PHP-specific benchmarks exist that test WordPress patterns (hooks, filters, block theme JSON, theme.json) -- all available benchmarks test generic algorithmic PHP, not framework-specific generation
Fine-tuning results for PHP/WordPress are anecdotal -- no peer-reviewed papers compare base vs fine-tuned open-source models specifically on WordPress code quality
Real-world PHP generation quality (PSR compliance, security, framework idioms) is not captured by HumanEval/MultiPL-E pass rates, which test algorithmic problem-solving, not production code patterns
